comment_text.sn32.57:1.6: The “gods” of the rainy thunderclouds are distinct from the “god” (singular) who sends rain. This is lost in translation, as I use the plural form “heavens” in the latter case. It seems the revels of the gods of the rainy clouds induce Indra to send forth rain.

root_text.sn32.57:1.2: Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn32.57:1.3: “ko nu kho, bhante, hetu, ko paccayo, yenekadā devo vassatī”ti?
root_text.sn32.57:1.6: ‘yannūna mayaṁ sakāya ratiyā vaseyyāmā’ti, tesaṁ taṁ cetopaṇidhimanvāya devo vassati.
root_text.sn32.57:1.7: Ayaṁ kho, bhikkhu, hetu, ayaṁ paccayo, yenekadā devo vassatī”ti.

translation_text.sn32.57:1.2: Seated to one side, that mendicant said to the Buddha:
translation_text.sn32.57:1.3: “Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes the heavens rain?”
translation_text.sn32.57:1.4: “Mendicant, there are what are called gods of the rainy thunderclouds.
translation_text.sn32.57:1.6: ‘Why don’t we revel in our own kind of enjoyment?’ Then, in accordance with their wish, the heavens rain down.
translation_text.sn32.57:1.7: This is the cause, this is the reason why sometimes the heavens rain.”
translation_text.sn32.57:2.5: The Linked Discourses on gods of the clouds are complete.
